205	## Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)
206	
207	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:
208	
209	- makes the reliance explicit and readable (a mathematician sees exactly which Mathlib result the route stands on);
210	- gives `leandag` a real node, so the `\uses{}` resolves (no broken/unknown ref) and the node's effort is **done** rather than ∞;
211	- keeps it clear how much the route leans on Mathlib vs. on original work.
212	
213	Write the anchor like any statement block, with three differences:
214	
215	```latex
216	\begin{lemma}[Hensel's lemma]
217	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}
218	  \lean{Mathlib.RingTheory.Henselian.foo}   % the REAL Mathlib declaration
219	  \mathlibok
220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
221	  Statement of the result in the project's notation.
222	\end{lemma}
223	```
224	
225	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
226	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
227	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
228	
229	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. A wrong or invented `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop will treat a non-existent result as available and skip proving a real gap. So:
230	
231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.

(Definition~\ref{def:jshriek_ou}). Both corepresent the same functor, so uniqueness of
  corepresenting objects identifies them.

  In detail, the argument is a corepresentability transport; \(j_!\mathcal{O}_V\) is never unfolded.
  Write \(A = j_!\mathcal{O}_V\). We exhibit two corepresentations of one and the same
  \(\mathrm{Type}\)-valued functor and invoke the uniqueness-up-to-isomorphism of a corepresenting
  object to obtain the stated isomorphism, with no adjunction-mate transposition or naturality square
  computed by hand. The common functor is built by chaining three identifications.
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item The adjunction underlying the equivalence \(\Phi\) presents the functor corepresented by
      \(\Phi.\mathrm{functor}(A)\) as the composite of \(\Phi.\mathrm{inverse}\) with the functor
      corepresented by \(A\).
    \item The corepresentability isomorphism of Lemma~\ref{lem:sectionsFunctorCorepIso}, whiskered by the
      forgetful functor to types, identifies the functor corepresented by \(A\) with the sections
      functor: it is \(\operatorname{sectionsFunctor} V\) as a \(\mathrm{Type}\)-valued functor. This
      rests on the corepresentability of \(\Gamma(V,-)\) by \(j_!\mathcal{O}_V\) of
      Definition~\ref{def:jshriek_ou}.
    \item Sections of a pushforward are, by definition, sections over the preimage open:
      \(\Phi.\mathrm{inverse} \circ \operatorname{sectionsFunctor} V =
      \operatorname{sectionsFunctor}(\varphi.\mathrm{inv}^{-1}V)\), an equality of functors. Reading
      Lemma~\ref{lem:sectionsFunctorCorepIso} at the open \(\varphi.\mathrm{inv}^{-1}V\) then exhibits
      this same functor as the one corepresented by \(j_!\mathcal{O}_{\varphi.\mathrm{inv}^{-1}V}\).
  \end{enumerate}
  Thus \(\Phi.\mathrm{functor}(A)\) and \(j_!\mathcal{O}_{\varphi.\mathrm{inv}^{-1}V}\) corepresent the
  same functor; the uniqueness of corepresenting objects up to isomorphism reflects this to the stated
  isomorphism of objects. The only geometric inputs are the two definitional equalities
  ``corepresented functor \(=\) sections functor'' and ``sections of a pushforward \(=\) sections over
  the preimage''.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Quasi-coherence of an iso-pushforward reduces to its slices]
  \label{lem:pushforward_iso_qcoh_of_slice_qcoh}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforward_iso_qcoh_of_slice_qcoh,
    AlgebraicGeometry.coversTop_preimage_of_iso}
  \uses{lem:isQuasicoherent_of_coversTop_mathlib, lem:nonempty_quasicoherentData_mathlib}
  Let \(\varphi : X \cong Y\) be an isomorphism of schemes,
  \(\Phi = \operatorname{pushforwardEquivOfIso}\varphi\) the induced equivalence, \(H :
  X.\mathrm{Modules}\), and \((U_i)_{i \in I}\) a family of opens covering \(X\). Then quasi-coherence
  of the pushforward \(\Phi.\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\, H\) reduces to quasi-coherence of its
  restrictions to the preimage-transported cover: if for every \(i\) the slice
  \[
    \bigl(\Phi.\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\, H\bigr).\operatorname{over}
      \bigl(\varphi.\mathrm{inv}^{-1}\,U_i\bigr)
  \]
  is quasi-coherent, then \(\Phi.\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\, H\) is quasi-coherent.
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:isQuasicoherent_of_coversTop_mathlib, lem:nonempty_quasicoherentData_mathlib}
  The preimages \(V_i = \varphi.\mathrm{inv}^{-1}\,U_i\) again cover \(Y\): the underlying map of
  \(\varphi.\mathrm{inv}\) is a homeomorphism, so a covering family of \(X\) pulls back to a covering
  family of \(Y\). Concretely, membership in \(\operatorname{Opens.grothendieckTopology}\) is preserved
  under the iso by transporting the covering sieve \(\operatorname{Sieve.ofObjects}\) along
  \(\varphi\) (the cover-transport step). With \((V_i)\) covering \(Y\) and each slice
  \((\Phi.\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\, H).\operatorname{over} V_i\) quasi-coherent by hypothesis,
  quasi-coherence of \(\Phi.\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\, H\) follows from the cover criterion
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:isQuasicoherent_of_coversTop_mathlib}); the family \((U_i)\) is supplied by the
  quasi-coherence datum of \(H\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:nonempty_quasicoherentData_mathlib}).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[The slice structure-sheaf ring map $\psi_r$]
  \label{lem:slice_structureSheaf_hom}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sliceStructureSheafHom}
  % NOTE: build target. The Lean declaration does not exist yet.
  Let \(\varphi : X \cong Y\) be an isomorphism of schemes, \(W \subseteq X\) an open and
  \(V = \varphi.\mathrm{inv}^{-1}\,W \subseteq Y\) its preimage. There is a morphism of sheaves of
  rings \(\psi_r\) over the slice (over-category) picture relating the structure sheaf of \(W\) to that
  of \(V\), obtained by restricting the structure-sheaf morphism \(\varphi.\mathrm{hom}\) induces
  between \(\mathcal{O}_X\) and \(\mathcal{O}_Y\) (its associated sheaf-of-rings morphism) to the
  over-category over \(W\). This single cross-ring slice structure-sheaf ring map is the datum from
  which the pullback functor of Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_slice_pullback_iso} is built.
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  The morphism \(\varphi.\mathrm{hom}\) carries a canonical map of structure sheaves; transport it to
  the over-category over \(W\) using the Beck--Chevalley identity that post-composition with a functor
  commutes with the forgetful functor out of the slice (the equality
  \(\operatorname{Over.post} F \circ \operatorname{Over.forget} = \operatorname{Over.forget} \circ F\)
  holds definitionally). The only non-definitional adjustment is the unit-isomorphism correction built
  into the over-category transport equivalence (the \(\operatorname{Over.map}\) along the unit
  isomorphism). The result is the slice structure-sheaf ring map \(\psi_r\) over \(V\).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Pullback along $\psi_r$ realizes the per-slice pushforward]
  \label{lem:pushforward_slice_pullback_iso}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardSlicePullbackIso}
  % NOTE: build target. The Lean declaration does not exist yet.
  \uses{lem:slice_structureSheaf_hom}
  Let \(\varphi : X \cong Y\), \(W \subseteq X\), \(V = \varphi.\mathrm{inv}^{-1}\,W\), and let
  \(\psi_r\) be the slice structure-sheaf ring map of Lemma~\ref{lem:slice_structureSheaf_hom}. Pulling
  back \(H.\operatorname{over} W\) (transported across the opens-equivalence) along \(\psi_r\) is
  isomorphic to the restricted pushforward:
  \[
    \bigl(\operatorname{pullback}\psi_r\bigr).\mathrm{obj}\bigl(H.\operatorname{over} W
      \text{ transported}\bigr)
      \;\cong\;
    \bigl(\Phi.\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\, H\bigr).\operatorname{over} V .
  \]
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:slice_structureSheaf_hom}
  Section by section the two sides agree. The pullback-unit isomorphism of the structure-sheaf pullback
  identifies \((\operatorname{pullback}\psi_r).\mathrm{obj}\) of the transported module with the
  pushforward restricted over \(V\), and the opens-equivalence identification
  ``\(W\) corresponds to \(\varphi.\mathrm{inv}^{-1}\,W = V\)'' holds definitionally. Combining the
  unit isomorphism with this Beck--Chevalley definitional equality gives the stated isomorphism.
\end{proof}

  image cover \(V_i = \varphi.\mathrm{inv}^{-1}\,U_i\) of \(Y\); it covers \(Y\) because the underlying
  map of \(\varphi.\mathrm{inv}\) is a homeomorphism, so the preimages of a cover form a cover (the image
  of an affine open under the scheme isomorphism is again affine,
  Lemma~\ref{lem:isAffineOpen_image_of_iso_mathlib}, when one wants the cover to remain affine). By
  Lemma~\ref{lem:isQuasicoherent_of_coversTop_mathlib} it suffices to show that
  \((\Phi.\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\, H).\operatorname{over} V_i\) is quasi-coherent for every \(i\).

  \emph{Per-member transport.} Fix \(i\) and let
  \(e_i : (X.\mathrm{Modules}\!\restriction\! U_i) \simeq (Y.\mathrm{Modules}\!\restriction\! V_i)\) be
  the opens-site equivalence induced by \(\varphi\) restricted to \(U_i\), built from
  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence_mathlib}. This \(e_i\) preserves colimits and carries
  the structure-sheaf unit to the structure-sheaf unit (it is a site equivalence whose underlying base
  is a homeomorphism), so by Lemma~\ref{lem:presentation_map_mathlib} the presentation \(p_i\) of
  \(H.\operatorname{over} U_i\) transports to a presentation of
  \(e_i.\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}(H.\operatorname{over} U_i)\). The comparison isomorphism of
  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_commutes_restriction},
  \[
    e_i.\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\bigl(H.\operatorname{over} U_i\bigr)
      \;\cong\;
    \bigl(\Phi.\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\, H\bigr).\operatorname{over} V_i,
  \]
  then carries that presentation, via Lemma~\ref{lem:presentation_ofIsIso_mathlib}, to a presentation of
  \((\Phi.\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\, H).\operatorname{over} V_i\). A sheaf of modules with a (global)
  presentation is quasi-coherent, so each member of the image cover is quasi-coherent, and the claim
  follows by Lemma~\ref{lem:isQuasicoherent_of_coversTop_mathlib}.

  This is the isomorphism case of the Stacks functoriality remark (``it is in general not the case that
  the pushforward of a quasi-coherent module is quasi-coherent''; here it holds because \(\varphi\) is an
  isomorphism, equivalently \(\varphi_* = (\varphi^{-1})^*\) is a pullback).
\end{proof}
input.new_string:
Quasi-coherence is a \emph{local} datum: it transports across the equivalence \(\Phi\) by moving
  \(H\)'s local presentations to the image cover, member by member. We never seek a global presentation
  of \(H\) (it has none — \(H\) is quasi-coherent only on the abstract source); we move the local
  presentations one open at a time. The carrying functor need not be the full
  restricted-module equivalence: a single \emph{left adjoint} pullback functor along one cross-ring
  slice structure-sheaf ring map suffices, which keeps the colimit-preservation that
  presentation-transport requires while collapsing the heavy equivalence bookkeeping to one ring map,
  one unit isomorphism, and one comparison isomorphism.

  \emph{The cover.} Extract \(H\)'s quasi-coherence datum
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:nonempty_quasicoherentData_mathlib}): a family \((U_i)_{i \in I}\) of opens covering
  \(X\) together with, for each \(i\), a presentation \(p_i\) of \(H.\operatorname{over} U_i\). Form the
  image cover \(V_i = \varphi.\mathrm{inv}^{-1}\,U_i\) of \(Y\) (the image of an affine open under the
  scheme isomorphism is again affine, Lemma~\ref{lem:isAffineOpen_image_of_iso_mathlib}, when one wants
  the cover to remain affine). By Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_iso_qcoh_of_slice_qcoh} it suffices to show
  that \((\Phi.\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\, H).\operatorname{over} V_i\) is quasi-coherent for every
  \(i\).

  \emph{Per-member transport.} Fix \(i\) and write \(W = U_i\), \(V = V_i =
  \varphi.\mathrm{inv}^{-1}\,W\). Let \(\psi_r\) be the slice structure-sheaf ring map of
  Lemma~\ref{lem:slice_structureSheaf_hom}, and consider the pullback functor
  \(\operatorname{pullback}\psi_r\). Being a left adjoint, it preserves colimits, so by
  Lemma~\ref{lem:presentation_map_mathlib} the presentation \(p_i\) of \(H.\operatorname{over} W\)
  (transported across the opens-equivalence) transports to a presentation of
  \((\operatorname{pullback}\psi_r).\mathrm{obj}(H.\operatorname{over} W \text{ transported})\). The
  comparison isomorphism of Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_slice_pullback_iso},
  \[
    \bigl(\operatorname{pullback}\psi_r\bigr).\mathrm{obj}\bigl(H.\operatorname{over} W
      \text{ transported}\bigr)
      \;\cong\;
    \bigl(\Phi.\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\, H\bigr).\operatorname{over} V,
  \]
  then carries that presentation, via Lemma~\ref{lem:presentation_ofIsIso_mathlib}, to a presentation of
  \((\Phi.\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\, H).\operatorname{over} V\). A sheaf of modules with a (global)
  presentation is quasi-coherent, so each member of the image cover is quasi-coherent, and the claim
  follows by Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_iso_qcoh_of_slice_qcoh}.

  This is the isomorphism case of the Stacks functoriality remark (``it is in general not the case that
  the pushforward of a quasi-coherent module is quasi-coherent''; here it holds because \(\varphi\) is an
  isomorphism, equivalently \(\varphi_* = (\varphi^{-1})^*\) is a pullback).
\end{proof}
